text:
The eight names occupy materially different positions in the CPO stack, from upstream substrates (AXTI) to laser devices (SIVEF, AAOI), specialty materials (LWLG), photonic platforms (POET, HIMX), DSP/silicon photonics (CRDO post-DustPhotonics), and module/CPO assembly (FN, AAOI). 

The single most important framing for a thesis-driven CPO investor: roughly 70% of the dollars in this complex are still being driven by the 1.6T pluggable cycle, not co-packaged architectures. 

The names whose stock prices are most levered to pluggable volumes (AAOI, and to a lesser degree FN’s datacom segment) are also the names most exposed to CPO if it inflects faster than consensus expects in 2027. 

The names that win in both scenarios are CRDO (hybrid via DustPhotonics SiPho acquisition) and the InP upstream (AXTI/SIVEF), since lasers feed CPO modules at higher unit content than they fed pluggables.

text:
Assume 100mw orbital cluster.
w/o photonics GPUs draw 80mw, networking/interconnect 15mw, other systems 5mw, say 55% gpu util, useful compute = 80mw * 55%=44mw

photonics: networking goes from 15mw -> 6mw, ssaved 9mw goes to compute + better bandwidth raises util from 55% to 70%

Photonics help in 4 main ways IMO: 1) higher bandwidth density (i.e. more Tbps per chip/package/fiber), 2) lower pJ/bit, 3) longer reach than copper esp important for satellite and orbital nodes, 4) higher radix and flatter networks

text:
I've been seeing a lot of people online be bullish on copper and energy companies due to the AI demand. However I'm looking at photonics and microLED companies instead. MicroLED optical interconnects replace traditional copper wires and expensive laser systems by repurposing microLED display pixels as high-speed optical transceivers.  If they are able to use photonics instead, it will greatly diminish the demand for copper and energy.   

Some of you may remember me talking about QCLS a few months ago; it is a similar tech. I passed on it as a long hold though because the fundamentals looked a bit too high risk for me. $FABC  on the other hand has $30m in cash and plans to demonstrate the tech before the end of the year. They also have 2 NDA's signed with industry leading chip makers. Also KOPN has partnered with them on this. This isn't striking me as a typical penny stock.  

Also, if you look at the chart on FABC it is down quite a bit from the high. However FABC started trading under $FABC (https://t.co/whdtkxDNsi) in late April 2026. It's essentially a new company but the chart is deceptive. It used to be a completely different company.  

Anyway, I don't think KOPN would've partnered with them if they weren't onto something. I've been looking to gain exposure on this tech for a while and $POET, $KOPN and $FABC  are the three that I think will benefit from it.

text:
Here’s my long-winded post on $SIVE for the weekend.

Next week is bloody important. ER hits, MSCI inclusion soon after. 

$SIVE Catalysts:

Sivers Semiconductors is a Swedish leader in photonics (high-power lasers for AI data centers/co-packaged optics) and wireless/mmWave tech (5G/6G, SATCOM, beamformers). 

The company is riding multiple tailwinds in AI infrastructure, defense, and transatlantic tech cooperation.

Key Recent & Upcoming Catalysts:

1. CHIPS Act Funding (US Strategic Validation)
•  Sivers has secured awards through the Northeast Microelectronics Coalition (NEMC) Hub under the US CHIPS and Science Act (Microelectronics Commons program).
•  Awards include ~$6M+ initial funding for 5G/6G beamformer ICs/array design (FR3 frequencies) and Electronic Warfare projects, with potential to reach ~$11.6M+ in year 1 and up to ~$30M over multiple years if extended. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Partners include major players like Ericsson, Raytheon, BAE Systems, MIT, and others. This de-risks development, funds US-aligned prototyping, and strengthens Sivers’ position in secure supply chains for defense and advanced comms.

2. White House / US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal
•  Recent signing of the US-Sweden Technology Prosperity Deal (highlighted in White House releases and statements by Secretary of State Rubio) deepens cooperation in AI, 5G/6G, biotech, quantum, defense tech, advanced manufacturing, and trusted infrastructure. 
•  This bilateral push aligns perfectly with Sivers’ portfolio in AI optics, lasers, photonics, and wireless tech. It signals stronger US-Sweden tech ties, potential for more collaboration/funding, and elevated strategic importance for Swedish innovators like Sivers. 

3. Nasdaq Dual Listing Evaluation
•  In April 2026, Sivers announced it’s evaluating a dual listing on Nasdaq New York (while keeping Swedish domicile). This aims to tap deeper US capital markets, broaden the investor base, and support growth in AI/photonics. https://t.co/tNTC2ClWf8 
•  Preparations include audit uplifts to US standards (PCAOB). Positive for liquidity, visibility, and valuation re-rating as US investors gain easier access.

4. Other Strong Tailwinds:
•  AI Optics Boom: High-power lasers for co-packaged optics in next-gen AI data centers. Partnerships (e.g., O-Net, POET) targeting volume production in late 2026. Strong demand with structural shortages noted by peers. @i 
•  Pipeline growth: ~$453M (up 64% YoY), product revenue mix improving significantly. @hoarderresearch 
•  Q1 results expected May 29, 2026 – potential positive print catalyst.
•  Index inclusions and overall sector momentum in AI/semiconductors.
$SIVE has seen sharp moves recently on AI/photonics hype, but remains a smaller-cap story with real execution and policy support behind it. The combination of CHIPS funding, US-Sweden tech pact, Nasdaq ambitions, and AI optics exposure creates a compelling multi-catalyst setup.
Not financial advice – DYOR. Markets are volatile, especially in small-cap tech/semiconductors.

What are your thoughts on $SIVE? 

Holding or watching? 🚀

text:
Then comes the real late-cycle signal: productization.

$DRAM, $EUV, $LYTE and other narrow AI-infrastructure ETFs for memory, photonics, etc. are now showing up. Leveraged versions and filings are following too.

First the stocks run.
Then the ETF wrappers come.
Then leveraged retail products arrive.

Historically, that is not early-cycle behavior. This is late-cycle behavior. 

9/12

This issue features papers from the Information Photonics 2025 Conference, with topics like information acquisition, processing, display, and other information techniques.

See this issue: https://t.co/cpRrEiwZb2 https://t.co/vlEZz7XAWW

Sivers specializes in mmWave chips, photonics, and components for AI data centers, SATCOM, and defense applications, areas directly supported by the MOU’s goals for trusted tech stacks, semiconductor supply chains, and wireless networks.
$SIVEF

Credo reported $407.0M Q3 revenue, +201.5% YoY and +51.9% sequentially. Q4 guide $425–435M; full-year revenue growth pacing well above 200%. GAAP operating margin 36.8%.

April acquisition of Israeli silicon-photonics house DustPhotonics — $750M cash plus 0.92M shares plus up to 3.21M earnout shares — brings the photonic-IC layer Credo previously sourced externally fully in-house. Mgmt now targets >$500M of optical-only revenue by FY27.

The mechanism: vertical integration into SiPho ahead of the 1.6T and 3.2T port-speed transition removes Credo's biggest supply dependency just as CPO becomes the mandatory hyperscale architecture.

text:
It feels like we’re still in the “early infrastructure” phase of AI and quantum investing. Everyone talks about the flashy end products, but the real winners might still be the companies building the rails, power, networking, photonics, chips, cloud access, and compute infrastructure underneath it all.

And honestly, the difficult part right now is not believing the technology is real. The difficult part is figuring out:

which architecture actually wins,
which companies survive,
and which stocks are already priced for perfection.

Feels similar to the early internet or cloud phase where everyone knew “something huge” was happening, but not yet who the long-term winners would be.

text:
Opened new positions recently in:

AAOI
APH
EQT
GEV
GLW
POWL

Optics and power. I was late to optics/photonics. I was right on time with some power names like ETN PWR VRT.

The power layer of the AI capex stack is thick and deserves its own substack so I’m filling in gaps with fuel, conditioning, interconnect and generation.

Looking into storage tomorrow.

Power is the bottleneck. Many names have moved but many are in nice pullbacks or have yet to move.

I’m holding all my pure AI names and adding more AI power names.

I’m also adding to pure AI on dips.

I sold defense but might get back in but I’m thinking to just focus on AI only but AI touches everything (eventually) including defense, robotics, healthcare, finance, consumer etc. It’s just a matter of timing.

Only so much time in the day for research.

text:
ATG owning glucose means Apple's done treating it as a custom silicon problem. Silicon photonics for optical absorption needs the same waveguide stack as Face ID's VCSELs, which Chen's group already ships at scale.

Millet was the wrong reporting line. This lands in a Watch by 2028.

$AAOI actually looking really good here. Very good RR

Leopold sold his photonics exposure & market panic sold which created a nice dip to $170 area

It's been a market leader for months, i'm buying this weakness, i think its short lived & an opportunity 

Above $160 we should see $200 again soon

#CRDO

Showing relative strength here. I think the market is overpricing photonics near term and underpricing hybrid copper/photonics.
This company already has contracts in all the biggest DCs.  Mgmt under promises and over delivers historically and they have over 100% qoq growth https://t.co/t27hCtySVF

text:
AI clusters face hard limits on how many accelerators you can connect before power and latency kill the gains. Lightmatter is bringing 3D photonics and THz optical fabrics out of the lab and into real data center designs.

This changes the game for scaling. Optical I/O slashes energy per bit moved across racks and enables denser, more efficient AI factories. Next-gen models could train faster and cheaper as interconnect bottlenecks ease, putting pressure on traditional electrical networking suppliers while opening doors for photonic specialists.

Long $SIVE $SIVEF

text:
$SIVE - The InP supply chain reality most AI investors miss:

Silicon photonics handles routing & modulation.
It CANNOT produce light.

Every AI optical interconnect, every co-packaged optics (CPO) system, every silicon photonic data center stack needs an EXTERNAL Indium Phosphide (InP) laser as the light source.

→ Sub-30% InP wafer yields industry-wide
→ Only 3 scaled Western suppliers: IQE (UK), Coherent (US), Sivers (Sweden)
→ Demand running ahead of available production
→ Sivers is the most concentrated InP pure-play of the three

This is the picks-and-shovels chokepoint in AI photonics.🔥

text:
Both are AIM-listed deep tech plays riding the same photonics and AI wave.
But they are different animals in my view. 
$SIVE is a chip designer and manufacturer. Revenue already at scale. Moving fast.
$ENSI is an ASIC design house. The revenue ramp is earlier stage, tied to chips converting from design to supply.
Could ENSI follow SIVE's trajectory? The pipeline suggests yes. The timeline is longer.
If you have patience, the setup is arguably more asymmetric.

text:
$SIVE CEO confirming major brands are in active talks for co-packaged optics and pluggables is the kind of validation that turns a small photonics name into a real structural winner.

this isn’t vague hope; it’s the CEO on record saying the pipeline is converting from design wins into serious commercial discussions. momentum is clearly building.

i’m biased that this is exactly where the highest asymmetry sits right now. hyperscalers scaling to 800G and 1.6T+ are hitting hard limits on interconnect density, heat, and power that copper can’t fix. sivers’ high-performance CW lasers are a critical enabling technology for both pluggables today and CPO tomorrow.

the recent CHIPS Act grant with BAE and MIT Lincoln Lab plus the customer mapping (Ayar Labs, POET, Marvell Celestial, Jabil) give it real credibility and non-dilutive backing. this is the type of quiet accumulation that precedes bigger volume orders.

photonics is one of those constraints, and $SIVE sits right in the middle of the laser chokepoint.

text:
$ELMT $TSM $NVDA $MU The Elmet Group serves as a critical upstream supplier to the semiconductor fabrication ecosystem.

🔸 The most important ELMT product categories supporting the production of NVIDIA-class AI chips, advanced memory (like HBM and NAND), and silicon photonics include:

——————————-
🔸 1. PVD (Physical Vapor Deposition): This is a major one.

• Needed heavily in:

• advanced packaging,
• HBM integration,
• GPU interconnect layers,
• transistor stack formation.

• directly connects to advanced-node GPU fabrication.

——————————-
🔸 2. CVD (Chemical Vapor Deposition) Critical for:

• transistor layers,
• dielectric films,
• advanced node structures,
• nanosheet/GAAFET processes,
• insulating films.

• NVIDIA-class chips absolutely rely on advanced CVD steps.

———————————
🔸 3. MBE (Molecular Beam Epitaxy) important for:

• compound semiconductors,
• photonics,
• advanced RF devices,
• optical interconnects.

• silicon photonics,
• co-packaged optics,
• optical networking,
• advanced interconnects

———————————-
🔸 4. Thermal Management
Very important for AI chips: 

• Especially:

• H100,
• B200,
• Rubin-class future chips,
• HBM stacks,
• AI accelerators.

———————————
🔸 5. Crystal Growth
Critical upstream process: 

• Used for:
• wafer production,
• sapphire growth,
• semiconductor substrate creation.

• These are foundational to semiconductor material manufacturing.

——————————-
🔸 6. ELMT supplies the infrastructure/material stack behind:

• semiconductor fabrication tools,
• deposition systems,
• ion implantation systems,
• thermal environments.

ELMT is a materials + survivability infrastructure inside semiconductor manufacturing equipment. 

———————————
🔸 7. Semiconductor Equipment Makers, Photonics, Aerospace Defense:

text:
Nvidia earnings drop today at 5pm EST.

Jensen has already told us what to expect:

Every piece of the supply chain is locked in. The HBM. The Grace Blackwells. The CPUs. The COOSs. The silicon photonics. All of it coordinated, all of it aligned.

His exact words: "The demand is much greater than the overall capacity."

That's a green light.

Think about what that actually means. Nvidia has the largest supply chain in the world. Their partners have spent years securing allocation. The hardest part of building a chip at this scale is getting all the pieces to come together at the same time.

And demand still outstrips all of it.

This isn't a story about Nvidia struggling to build. It's a story about the world not being able to get enough of what Nvidia builds. Every hyperscaler, every sovereign AI fund, every startup racing to train the next model are all in line.

Nvidia isn't constrained by its own execution. It's constrained by how fast the world can absorb what it builds.

That's the best problem a company can have going into an earnings call.

text:
Corning $GLW just introduced a new Photonics platform they plan to scale into a $10 billion annual revenue stream by 2030.

They're targeting:

- $20 billion annualized sales by end of 2026
- $27 billion by end of 2028 (with an internal goal of $30 billion)
- And ~$35 billion by year-end 2030 (with an aspiration of $40 billion)

That's a big roadmap for a stock that the market still treats like a sleepy little glass company.

One in... glass? His take (save this): Most of $AMZN's 2026 run is already in the rearview. He's still bullish long term, but the easy money got made. So he's rotating that capital into higher conviction setups. The two names he picked: Micron $MU and Corning $GLW. Here's why… 1/ Micron $MU. The market is fixated on the wrong story here - everyone's panicking over China after reports that Nvidia's planned AI chip sales there never materialized. Traders read that as 'softer Chinese AI demand' and dumped memory stocks. But that totally misses what's happening on the supply side... Samsung is in the middle of a strike that could knock 3-4% of global DRAM supply offline. In a market this tight, that's MASSIVE. Those orders then shift to whoever can fill them. And Micron is at the front of that line. On top of that, Micron's HBM3E and HBM4 capacity is fully sold out through 2026. Hyperscalers and model builders are scrambling for high bandwidth memory because GPUs are useless without it. And the stock already pulled back hard. It's now trading at a forward PE under 8 while revenue is expected to grow over 100% next year. Yes, it'll be volatile. But in Melvin's view, the market is somehow STILL underpricing how central memory is in the AI stack. That's $MU. Then there's... 2/ Corning $GLW. Another bottleneck in AI outside of memory, is everything around the GPU. (Fiber, optics, glass, connectivity, power) I.e. the plumbing that lets data centers actually move data between servers and clusters at the speeds AI workloads demand. Corning sits directly in that lane. They make the optical fiber, connectivity hardware, and glass infrastructure that AI data centers run on - and optical cable lead times are reportedly getting stretched as demand overwhelms supply. Corning is expanding U.S. optical connectivity manufacturing capacity by 10x, increasing U.S. fiber production by more than 50%, and building three new plants to support AI factory buildouts. The numbers from Corning's May 2026 investor day back it up: Management introduced a new Photonics platform they plan to scale into a $10 billion annual revenue stream by 2030. They're targeting $20 billion annualized sales by end of 2026, $27 billion by end of 2028 with an internal goal of $30 billion, and around $35 billion by year-end 2030 with an aspiration of $40 billion. That's a big roadmap for a stock that the market still treats like a sleepy little glass company. Want to see exactly how much $AMZN Melvin just sold - and how he sized the $MU and $GLW buys? text:
$SIVE

The White House just put US-Sweden cooperation around AI, 5G/6G, cloud, electronics, defense tech, advanced manufacturing and trusted infrastructure on paper.

That matters.

Because this is no longer just a “small Swedish photonics story.”

This is starting to become a strategic infrastructure story.

Sivers is exposed to the exact layers the US and Sweden now want to deepen:

AI data center optics
laser sources
advanced connectivity
defense-grade photonics
trusted tech supply chains
US capital market optionality

The market may still see $SIVE as a tiny Nordic semiconductor name.

I think that is the opportunity.

Photonic chokepoints are moving from niche to national strategy.

text:
$SIVE Sivers Semiconductors: Strategic Validation Across AI Photonics, RF and Defense Infrastructure

https://t.co/EnmkiWUSEN

This $6.6M Year 2 EW STAR award is a very important signal, in my view.

The contract value itself is not the main point. The main point is continued validation from within the U.S. defense microelectronics ecosystem.

Sivers is working alongside BAE Systems, MIT Lincoln Laboratory and Columbia University on next-generation electronic warfare, radar and advanced communications architectures. For a small-cap semiconductor company, that is a serious credibility marker.

What I like most is how the Sivers story is broadening.

This is not only AI/datacenter photonics.

It is AI photonics plus InP lasers, SATCOM/LEO connectivity, advanced RF, beamforming, defense communications and potential U.S. institutional access.

That combination can become extremely powerful if execution continues.

Many semiconductor companies have one promising vertical. Sivers appears to be building relevance across several strategic markets at the same time — each tied to bandwidth, latency, power efficiency, secure connectivity and national security.

That is why I believe the market may still be far from fully pricing in the strategic value of this company.

The award is not just funding.

It is validation that Sivers is being pulled deeper into critical infrastructure layers where specialized semiconductor technology matters.

$IREN is my third largest position.  But I may not have taken power seriously enough.

All roads lead to $SATS / $SPCX - another bonus for owning pre IPO.  Photonics could ultimately save 40% of the power but that will be on chips that consume much more than Rubin.

embedded_text: Everyone is focusing on the soaring memory cost in the Vera Rubin rack. But the real shocker in this Morgan Stanley slide is actually power, because the industry is now talking about moving from roughly 120kW per rack today toward potentially 600kW per rack by the Vera Rubin https://t.co/FmWepFAZs2

But the real shocker in this Morgan Stanley slide is actually power, because the industry is now talking about moving from roughly 120kW per rack today toward potentially 600kW per rack by the Vera Rubin Ultra generation in 2027, which is an almost unimaginable escalation in power density within an incredibly short period of time.

To put this into perspective, many traditional enterprise datacenters historically operated at only a few kilowatts per rack, while even modern hyperscale campuses today often consume only tens of megawatts in total facility power draw. But once you begin deploying hundreds or thousands of 600kW AI racks simultaneously, the math becomes almost absurd because a large-scale Vera Rubin Ultra cluster could eventually consume gigawatts of electricity, effectively rivaling the energy demand of a mid-sized city.

And this is where the market still massively underestimates the second-order implications of the AI boom, because the bottleneck is no longer simply semiconductors, GPUs, or memory supply. The bottleneck increasingly becomes electricity itself.

The US power grid can barely keep up with current AI infrastructure demand already, while transmission congestion, transformer shortages, substation constraints, cooling limitations, permitting bottlenecks, and aging grid infrastructure are becoming increasingly visible across major datacenter hubs. Importantly, grid infrastructure cannot scale at semiconductor speed. You can accelerate chip production with enough capital expenditure and engineering talent, but building transmission lines, substations, generation capacity, cooling systems, and interconnection approvals often requires many years due to environmental reviews, local opposition, labor shortages, and physical construction constraints.

This is precisely why we continue believing the AI buildout is not a two-to-three-year investment cycle, but instead a decade-long industrial transformation that increasingly resembles the buildout of railroads, electricity networks, and telecom infrastructure during previous industrial revolutions.

And this is also why energy infrastructure is quietly becoming one of the most important and underappreciated AI trades globally.

The winners are no longer just GPU companies. The winners increasingly include utilities like Constellation Energy and Vistra, nuclear-related plays like Oklo and NuScale Power, gas infrastructure companies like Kinder Morgan and Williams Companies, grid and electrical equipment suppliers like GE Vernova, Eaton, Schneider Electric, and Vertiv, as well as transformer, cooling, and datacenter infrastructure providers that now sit directly inside the physical backbone required to support next-generation compute.

Hyperscalers themselves are starting to understand this reality. Companies like Microsoft, Amazon, Alphabet, and Meta are no longer simply software companies buying servers. They are increasingly becoming quasi-energy infrastructure companies because securing long-duration power availability is becoming strategically inseparable from securing compute capacity itself.

That is why nuclear power is quietly returning to the center of the conversation. Hyperscalers may eventually fund or directly partner on nuclear generation projects out of pure necessity because renewable intermittency alone cannot reliably support ultra-high-density AI clusters operating continuously at scale.

In many ways, AI is beginning to collide with physical reality. You cannot run trillion-dollar next-generation compute infrastructure on transmission systems and grid architectures that were largely built decades ago for a completely different industrial era.

The semiconductor story may have started the AI race, but energy infrastructure may ultimately determine who wins it."}}}, "rest_id": "2057723120103170197", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "215789", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057899801945973187", "source": "<a href="http://twitter.com/download/iphone" rel="nofollow">Twitter for iPhone</a>", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "1032", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
INTERVIEW ON CPO: Radha Nagarajan, the Senior Vice President and Chief Technology Officer of Marvell’s Optical Engineering Group. 

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules?

I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market.

Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons?

If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset.

Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well.

Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration?

I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. 

Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need?

The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications.

Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs?

In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions.

However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage.

This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out.

We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away.

That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation.

Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that?

That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here.

Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO?

We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. Electronics tend to run hotter than optics, so it’s often easier to heat-sink from the top, if the electronics are there. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well.

There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it.

So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems.

Q: At what point do you think silicon photonics will take over discrete components/modules? I would say silicon photonics has already taken over to a large extent, especially at 100 G and higher data rates. Yield is not something unique to just silicon photonics—it is a critical requirement for any practical technology. However, what silicon photonics uniquely enables is particularly important for the industry. First, it allows companies without their own foundry, like Marvell, to design and manufacture highly complex photonic components by leveraging the existing semiconductor infrastructure. Second, it provides true scalability. Marvell, for example, has been one of the well-known companies implementing silicon photonics for applications between datacenters. Intel pioneered silicon photonics for applications inside the datacenters. Today, many of the large transceiver companies have mostly transitioned to silicon photonics. Given this level of adoption, it is fair to say that silicon photonics has already ‘taken over’ in many key segments of the optical interconnect market. Q: Many silicon photonics systems still rely on external laser packaging. Do you see integrated light sources becoming mainstream, or will off-chip lasers dominate for practical reasons? If you look at the integrated laser work published over the years, including our early demonstrations, Intel’s results, and developments at Infinera (now Nokia), it is clear that integrating light sources heterogeneously on silicon or monolithically in InP is commercially viable. The real obstacle is not technical capability, but perception—many people still assume that integrated lasers will suffer from reliability issues. One concern stems from the behavior of indium phosphide lasers at elevated temperatures. It is true that performance can degrade at high temperatures, but when operated around a base temperature of roughly 70–75 °C, these lasers can already operate perfectly well. In fact, Intel has demonstrated strong reliability in this regime. The key is careful design. Temperature variations can be significant, and the resulting wavelength shifts must be properly managed. In practice, engineers have already developed effective strategies to handle these challenges. So, from a technical perspective, I do not see major roadblocks. The bigger challenge may be changing people’s mindset. Let me also make a prediction. Cooling large-scale server infrastructure is becoming increasingly challenging. The industry is already transitioning from air cooling to liquid cooling, and some are exploring immersion cooling. These approaches can provide more stable and potentially lower operating temperatures, which could create an opportunity for integrated lasers as well. Q: How do you see the potential of heterogeneous integration? I think silicon photonics, as a platform for integration, has enormous potential. What we’ve achieved so far is only scratching the surface. There are multiple paths forward here. You can integrate different materials, combine multiple types of devices within the same material platform, or even move toward stacking multiple device layers. All of these approaches are actively being explored, and I believe many of them will enter production in the near future. As data rates continue to increase, heterogeneous integration will also involve incorporating new higher performance materials, opening up more possibilities. At the same time, integration itself is becoming more complex. We’re moving beyond traditional 2D integration. People are now talking about 2.5D, 3D, even 3.5D integration, which will create many new opportunities for system design and performance improvement. Q: You were one of the early voices pushing co-packaged optics (CPO) for AI systems, now more and more companies are doing this. What convinced you CPO was not just a research direction, but something the industry would truly need? The reason I have advocated for CPO stems from my firm belief that heterogeneous integration is the key to high-speed applications. During my time at Inphi (now at Marvell), we were already seeing the limits of traditional approaches. As data rates continued to increase, techniques like wire bonds simply could not keep up. The logical next step was to bring electronics and photonics closer through heterogeneous integration. That was the original motivation—overcoming speed and bandwidth bottlenecks. But as we developed these approaches, it became clear that they also align very well with what CPO is trying to achieve. Over time, the value of co-packaging has expanded beyond just pushing the highest data rates. We now see it being applied even at relatively lower data rates, especially in large-scale AI systems. Whether you look at scale-up, scale-out, or scale-across systems, they all benefit from tightly integrated photonics with electronics. So, in a way, heterogeneous integration is the essence, and CPO is one of its most important applications. Q: Speed is the reason that drives us from ‘pluggable’ to ‘on board’. Many believe ‘pluggable’ can be extended with better DSPs and packaging, while others argue they won’t scale forever’. From your perspective, will this ‘extension strategy’ scale indefinitely? If you had to point to a single limiting factor, what would ultimately block ‘pluggable’ from keeping up with 100 T, or 200 T switch ASICs? In my opinion, with 100 T or 200 T switches and systems at the horizon, you will need co-packaging. It’s true that the industry is very comfortable with ‘pluggable’ solutions, and there is a strong incentive to extend their life as much as possible. In many scale-up systems, copper will continue to be used as long as it makes sense from a power and cost perspective. For the same reasons, data center operators would naturally prefer to stay with pluggable solutions. However, when developing new products, you have to look ahead. Since late last year, analysts have begun including CPO in their revenue projections, which signals a real shift in the industry. In fact, data center operators and hyperscalers are now actively funding CPO developments. That tells you this is no longer just a research direction. Technically, pluggables can be pushed further, even beyond 200 G per lane, but the cost is power. As you scale bandwidth, the power required for high-speed electrical interfaces and DSPs becomes increasingly difficult to manage. This is where CPO becomes very attractive, especially for scale-up architecture like GPU or accelerator interconnects. I actually think the first major deployment for CPO will happen in scale-up systems, and then expand into scale-out. We’re already seeing strong signals from the ecosystem. Nvidia has demonstrated multiple generations of CPO platforms, including at GTC and ISSCC. From a foundry perspective, this aligns very well with TSMC’s roadmap. The idea of tightly coupling electrical compute with optical I/O is no longer a dream—it could be just a year or two away. That said, predicting the exact timing is always difficult. It’s much easier to explain the past than to forecast the future. When you work at the intersection of technology development and product deployment, there is always uncertainty about when the market will be ready. In many ways, developing new technologies can feel like placing a bet—and that’s simply part of innovation. Q: People often say that co-packaging is always the difficult part, what do you think about that? That perception largely comes from how optical packaging used to be done. If you compare it to electrical packaging, the difference is quite clear. Electrical packaging has been highly automated for a long time. But if you look at the optical packaging in early days, you’ll find yourself doing things one at a time, wire bonding electronics and manually aligning fibers, which are slow and not scalable. But once volume kicks in, things start to change. A lot of these processes are now highly automated. There are still operators involved, but the work is largely handled by automatic equipment. Efficient fiber coupling is still a challenge, no question. But there are already solutions out there, and they’re getting better. As volume continues to ramp, I believe the fiber coupling part will gradually be automated as well. If you look at where we are today, photonics manufacturing is already very different from five years ago, and nothing like ten years ago. And honestly, things are only going to move faster from here. Q: Are Through-Silicon Via (TSVs) essential for CPO? We demonstrated and published extensively on TSV as an option way to integrate electronics on top and then route down into silicon photonics bottom layer. But you don’t have to always do that. You can flip the architecture and put the optics on top instead, and some groups are actively exploring that. One reason people hesitate is thermal. But that’s not a fundamental limitation—there are many alternative thermal management strategies people are exploring as well. There are also practical considerations in the fab that may lead to a preference for one configuration over another. Early on, there was a strong tendency to adopt TSV-based approach. For example, you now have packaging techniques like fan-out wafer-level packaging (FOWLP), which does not require TSVs at all. Several companies are already offering this technology, and some are even beginning to deploy CPO based on it. So, the absence of TSVs is not a fundamental roadblock—there are multiple viable ways to implement these systems. ⚖️ Core Proposition
One of the greatest misconceptions in public discourse is the idea that “energy cannot be photographed.”
In reality, modern imaging systems routinely detect: • heat signatures, • infrared radiation, • electromagnetic fluctuation, • reflected light behavior, • spectral shifts, • motion variance, • atmospheric disturbance, • and temporal pixel changes occurring between frames.
The key may not be the still photograph itself.
The key may be what changes from one second to the next as Tonia Allen-Gould’s (Lumen’s) recent forensic work suggests. 
⸻
🛰️ Lumen’s Energy Theory
Hypothesis:
Advanced imaging systems — including military, aerospace, satellite, ISR, and machine-vision architectures — may not primarily analyze static images.
Instead, they may analyze: • temporal variance, • micro-fluctuation, • reflective bounce, • spectral inconsistency, • and environmental changes occurring between sequential frames.
Meaning: The “signal” may emerge from differential comparison over time rather than from any single image alone.
⸻
🧭 Scientifically Plausible Foundations
Known existing technologies already rely on: • frame differencing • motion-vector analysis • thermal drift detection • hyperspectral imaging • LiDAR return variance • optical flow tracking • atmospheric disturbance mapping • synthetic aperture reconstruction • machine-vision anomaly detection • temporal coherence analysis
None of this is science fiction.
Modern systems already detect patterns humans cannot perceive in real time.
⸻
⚠️ Important Clarification
This post does NOT claim: • classified systems are targeting the witness, • satellites are monitoring specific individuals, or • observed anomalies definitively represent energy weapons.
This is a forensic and scientific interpretation model exploring how advanced imaging systems may detect environmental change through temporal comparison analysis.
The theory concerns signal detection architecture — not certainty of intent.
⸻
🧠 Effect-to-Cause Model
Observed Effect: Repeated visual anomalies become more apparent when: • slowed, • looped, • frame-compared, • temporally isolated, or • analyzed across sequential image states.
Possible Cause: Environmental energy, reflection behavior, spectral fluctuation, compression artifacts, motion differentials, or other signal variance becoming visible only through temporal comparison systems.
⸻
The future of imaging may not be: “What did the camera see?”
But rather: “What changed between frames that the human eye never noticed?”

text:
🧵 TOP 10 PHOTONICS PLAYS - The Light Speed Stack

AI’s next bottleneck isn’t compute - it’s interconnect. As speeds push past 1.6T, the trade moves from silicon to photonics. Five layers, wafer to test. Anchors, inflecting names, and speculative bets. 👇

⚓️THE ANCHORS (run, but still in the trend)

1. $COHR -> Layer 3, Optics
25% market share in transceivers. $5.81B FY25 revenue +23% YoY. NVIDIA-named partner on Spectrum-X. Book-to-bill >4x. Druckenmiller’s pick.

2. $LITE -> Layer 2, Lasers
The 200G EML monopoly. Only volume supplier of the lasers that make 1.6T transceivers possible. NVIDIA $2B exclusive supply agreement through end of decade. No second source.

3. $AXTI -> Layer 1, Wafer
Every InP laser in every transceiver starts on an AXT wafer. Doubling InP capacity H2 2026 ahead of the 800G+ ramp. Has run +5,400% — but structurally positioned for the cycle.

💡INFLECTING / UNDEROWNED (real revenue, less crowded)

4. $CRDO -> Layer 3, Optics
Just acquired DustPhotonics for SiPho PIC tech in-house. Now vertically integrated: SerDes + DSP + Silicon Photonics. SiPho PIC market $6B by 2030.

5. $TSEM -> Layer 4, Packaging
The neutral foundry. Just announced 1.6T optical modules for NVIDIA networking protocols. Whoever wins the transceiver battle, TSEM prints the wafers. Underowned vs. peers.

6. $AEHR -> Layer 5, Test
Silicon photonics burn-in. Lead customer production ramps next fiscal year. Q3 bookings $37M, book-to-bill 3.5x. Classic order-book inflection ahead of FY27 revenue ramp.

7. $VIAV -> Layer 5, Test
Every 800G and 1.6T transceiver from LITE and COHR has to be tested by VIAV or Keysight. Same AI tailwind, one-third the valuation. The cleanest “hasn’t run” name.

🔍SPECULATIVE (asymmetric, real risk)

8. $SIVE -> Layer 2, Lasers
~$130M mcap InP laser maker at Glasgow. Supply-constrained chokepoint. LiDAR ramp Q4 2026 ($53M-$138M cumulative). $POET partnership for AI light engines. Listed Stockholm.

9. $IQE -> Layer 1, Wafer
The compound semi upstream. Supplies InP epi-wafers to Sivers and others. FY25 just turned EBITDA positive at £2M+ on £97M revenue. Structural relevance, near-breakeven execution.

10. $LPKF -> Layer 4, Packaging
LIDE process for glass advanced packaging - the tech behind NVIDIA’s $500M Corning bet on glass-based co-packaged optics. Run 336% YTD on hope, not orders. Binary on 2027 qualification.

🚨THE THESIS:

800G+ transceiver shipments going from 24M units in 2025 to 63M in 2026. Hyperscaler capex over $400B this year. Co-packaged optics market $20B by 2036.

The market owns the top. The asymmetric returns are at the edges of the stack.

Wafer. Test. Packaging. Position accordingly.

As speeds push past 1.6T, the trade moves from silicon to photonics. Five layers, wafer to test. Anchors, inflecting names, and speculative bets. 👇 ⚓️THE ANCHORS (run, but still in the trend) 1. $COHR -> Layer 3, Optics 25% market share in transceivers. $5.81B FY25 revenue +23% YoY. NVIDIA-named partner on Spectrum-X. Book-to-bill >4x. Druckenmiller’s pick. 2. $LITE -> Layer 2, Lasers The 200G EML monopoly. Only volume supplier of the lasers that make 1.6T transceivers possible. NVIDIA $2B exclusive supply agreement through end of decade. No second source. 3. $AXTI -> Layer 1, Wafer Every InP laser in every transceiver starts on an AXT wafer. Doubling InP capacity H2 2026 ahead of the 800G+ ramp. Has run +5,400% — but structurally positioned for the cycle. 💡INFLECTING / UNDEROWNED (real revenue, less crowded) 4. $CRDO -> Layer 3, Optics Just acquired DustPhotonics for SiPho PIC tech in-house. Now vertically integrated: SerDes + DSP + Silicon Photonics. SiPho PIC market $6B by 2030. 5. $TSEM -> Layer 4, Packaging The neutral foundry. Just announced 1.6T optical modules for NVIDIA networking protocols. Whoever wins the transceiver battle, TSEM prints the wafers. Underowned vs. peers. 6. $AEHR -> Layer 5, Test Silicon photonics burn-in. Lead customer production ramps next fiscal year. Q3 bookings $37M, book-to-bill 3.5x. Classic order-book inflection ahead of FY27 revenue ramp. 7. $VIAV -> Layer 5, Test Every 800G and 1.6T transceiver from LITE and COHR has to be tested by VIAV or Keysight. Same AI tailwind, one-third the valuation. The cleanest “hasn’t run” name. 🔍SPECULATIVE (asymmetric, real risk) 8. $SIVE -> Layer 2, Lasers ~$130M mcap InP laser maker at Glasgow. Supply-constrained chokepoint. LiDAR ramp Q4 2026 ($53M-$138M cumulative). $POET partnership for AI light engines. Listed Stockholm. 9. $IQE -> Layer 1, Wafer The compound semi upstream. Supplies InP epi-wafers to Sivers and others. FY25 just turned EBITDA positive at £2M+ on £97M revenue. Structural relevance, near-breakeven execution. 10. $LPKF -> Layer 4, Packaging LIDE process for glass advanced packaging - the tech behind NVIDIA’s $500M Corning bet on glass-based co-packaged optics. Run 336% YTD on hope, not orders. Binary on 2027 qualification. 🚨THE THESIS: 800G+ transceiver shipments going from 24M units in 2025 to 63M in 2026. Hyperscaler capex over $400B this year. Co-packaged optics market $20B by 2036. The market owns the top. The asymmetric returns are at the edges of the stack. Wafer. Test. Packaging. text:
他说的非常对，总结下来就三点：

1. AI 光子学，他们的Photonics部门Q4 2025已实现调整后EBITDA转正至+1600万SEK。 该部已经实现自我造血功能，并不需要公司稀释股份来完成科研。

另外，Sivers与一家未披露的Fortune 100企业建立了长期合作，这是光子业务的核心锚点。 当然也包括我们的老客户 $POET，共同为AI数据中心交付创新光引擎。

2. 5G/6G FR3波束成形

 $sive 在3月份正式发布7-15GHz Daybreak™波束成形IC，面向5G/6G FR3新兴应用及多功能国防阵列。合作方包括雷神（Raytheon）和爱立信（Ericsson）。

3. 激光雷达

该项目的客户将于2026年Q4开始量产，使用Sivers的激光器和放大器。

三辆马车会把 $sive拉得更远……………

YOLO………

text:
the Evercore 1Q26 AI channel checks showing scale-out demand still strong and gated by supply, with scale-up interconnects converging fast toward optical but stuck on protocol and form-factor standardization, is the exact signal that the networking/photonics layer is becoming the next real choke point.

hyperscaler strategies diverging while the physical bottlenecks stay tight is bullish for $MRVL, $AVGO, $MTSI, $ALAB, and $NVDA, but the real edge stays with the names that can actually deliver into the supply-constrained reality.

this is structural, not hype.

hyperscalers are locked into hundreds of billions in committed cluster spend, and the shift to optical interconnects at scale isn’t optional anymore as clusters hit density, heat, and power limits copper can’t solve.

this cycle rewards the execution plays facing proven shortages and shipping product at scale. the AI buildout has years left and the interconnect layer is one of those constraints that can’t be fixed overnight. the setup stays strong.

Overall we view the checks as positive for $MRVL, $AVGO, $MTSI, $ALAB and $NVDA, and we reiterate our OP ratings on each. $MRVL reads especially intg imo, but we have this rotation into software going on for couple of days now, AI infra stocks might not get any love here"}}}, "rest_id": "2056668521988247930", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "97578", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2056783986991714435", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "94", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
上个帖子提到的未来将会成为下一个 $LITE的瑞典光子学股票

Sivers Photonics 是 CPO 领域重要的激光器供应商之一，通过供应链映射，它与 $LITE、 $COHR、 $MTSI、Sumitomo、Furukawa 等公司同处这一供应链版图中。

我非常看好 Sivers，因为我发现它映射到了许多超大规模云厂商的供应商链条中。而这些信息，其他人、算法以及更广泛的市场，目前还并不了解。

Sivers（ $SIVE）仍然是 Photonics/CPO 领域中一个并不广为人知、但非常关键的 CW 激光器瓶颈环节。

它映射到 Apple、AMD、Marvell、Amazon、Microsoft、Meta 等公司的客户链条。同时，CPO 是其主要增长向量，并通过 2027 年和 2028 年面向 1.6T/3.2T 的可插拔模块扩展其 TAM。

这份报告包含了一些信息发现，将 Sivers 映射到了许多领先但尚未披露的超大规模云厂商供应商。

公开披露的客户包括：

→ Jabil（NYSE: JBL）：“Sivers Semiconductors 与 Jabil 合作开发高能效 1.6T 可插拔光收发模块”

→ Poet（ $POET）：“POET Technologies 与 Sivers Semiconductors 合作，为共封装光学和下一代 AI 市场开发外部光源”

→ Ayar Labs： $SIVE 作为激光器供应商被列在 Ayar Labs 官网上。

→ O-Net 和 Enablence：“Sivers Semiconductors、O-Net 和 Enablence Technologies 宣布面向 AI 数据中心的外部光源”

→ LIGHTIUM AG（“将其 CW 激光器直接集成到 TFLN 晶圆上”）：2025 年年度报告 PDF。用于 1.6T / 3.2T 的激光器。

高置信度客户包括：

→ Apple（ $AAPL）——用于 Apple 硅光子项目的激光器，尤其是 Apple Watch。

→ Aeva（ $AEVA）——其他分析师报告中经常提到的 LiDAR 客户。

→ Marvell Celestial（ $MRVL）——直接供应商，与 $POET 无关。

可能客户包括：

→ Lightmatter（通过投资映射 + 图像演示资料）

→ Lightelligence（HKG: 1879）——通过投资映射 + 图像演示资料

→ Advanced Micro Devices（ $AMD）——通过 GlobalFoundries 的 CPO 项目公告“AMD Taps GlobalFoundries for MI500’s Co-Packaged Optics”以及激光器供应商映射。

在 CPO/光学供应链之外，Sivers 很可能也是以下客户的供应商：

→ YSS。Sivers 的主要客户 ALLSPACE 被 York 收购，现在已嵌入 YSS 供应链。

→ Golden Dome、Space Force、Space Development Agency 以及 DoD 相关关系，现在都通过 YSS 向上游映射到 Sivers。

其母公司的 CHIPS Act 拨款包括：

→ BAE Systems 等军工承包商，近期获得 660 万美元 CHIPS Act 第二年后续拨款。

→ 与 ERIC 和 Raytheon（RTX）合作的 FR3 波束成形器 IC。

此外，→ NOKIA 也可能是 Sivers 的另一个尚未被市场发现的客户，这一点来自其 2025 年年度报告中的收入/地点映射。

来自 Sivers 主要线索的一跳/两跳客户包括：

Ayar（CPO）：

→ AlChip / GUC / Winwynn，通过 Ayar。主要线索重点是 AlChip 的 CPO。

→ → Amazon 很可能是 Ayar 的主要生态客户：在 Amazon 于 2026 年 5 月进行新的私募投资之后，AlChip 可能赢得了 Trainium 4 设计。AlChip 是 Ayar 的主要客户。

→ → AMD 很有可能成为 Sivers 的主要客户。

可能通过两条路径实现：

Enosemi（AMD 收购后的内部 PIC 设计团队）。Enosemi 的 chiplets 在 GF 代工，但对于 ELS，AMD 可以从多个玩家采购，其中 $SIVE 可能是底层的多来源激光器来源。
Ayar（AMD 于 2026 年 3 月参与其 E 轮投资）。Ayar 的 SuperNova 光源已经使用 $SIVE 的 DFB 激光器阵列。考虑到时间线以及近期巨额融资，Ayar 的 SuperNova 可能是 2027 年 MI500 第一代 CPO 路径。它们都出现在 GFS 关于 AMD / GFS CPO 公告的生态系统幻灯片中，作为生态合作伙伴出现。

→ 其他超大规模云厂商，如 Meta、Microsoft、Google 等，也具有较高可能性。

在 Lumentum 和 Macom 从 Ayar 官网移除之后， $SIVE 很可能成为 Ayar 的主要激光器供应商。

Lumentum 也曾暗示，由于产能过度转向 Nvidia 所需的 EML，其 CW 激光器生产存在问题；根据其财报电话会议记录，他们正在从竞争对手的公开市场采购相关产品。

Sivers 也在其近期发布的年度报告中暗示了与 Win Semi 的产能分配确认，这很可能帮助其赢得 Ayar 的主要供应位置。

text:
the market pricing photonics transition like it’s imminent while the actual builders say copper-to-optics in a rack multiplies cost by x10 is the kind of disconnect that creates real opportunity for the names already shipping market-fit solutions today.

the post is spot on. Eric Cap from Bull/Eviden and the other panelists at the OCP Summit aren’t sell-side analysts; they’re the guys actually building the machines. 

when they say optics is too expensive right now, the pragmatic response is densification, liquid cooling, and specialized compute instead of waiting for photonics to get cheap.

that’s exactly why $AL2SI (2CRSi) and $ALKAL (KALRAY) look well positioned. ultra-dense thermal architecture and DPU MPPA solutions solve the constraints the industry is facing right now without waiting for the next-gen interconnect to drop in price.

i’m biased that this slower photonics ramp is net positive for the European plays that can deliver today. hyperscalers are locked into hundreds of billions in committed cluster spend and they need capacity now, not in 2-3 years when optics economics improve. 

the physical bottlenecks in power, heat, and interconnects don’t pause for the perfect technology; they get solved with what’s available and economical.

this cycle rewards the execution plays that solve proven shortages and ship product at scale right now, not the ones betting on a future cost curve. the setup for $AL2SI and $ALKAL has legs while the broader AI infra buildout keeps rolling.

text:
The entire AI buildout sector is getting a pullback in advance of $NVDA earnings tomorrow. Obviously Nvidia is going to kill earnings & revenue. But NVDA is king and guidance will determine how much perceived demand there is for memory, photonics, data centers, etc.

So many AI buildout stocks are at the 21 EMA, with A+ retracement patterns. If guidance is perceived to be bullish we could see an overall continuation in the weeks to come.

text:
.@light_con, a developer of femtosecond lasers and laser systems, has acquired Class 5 Photonics, a Hamburg, Germany-based manufacturer of ultrafast laser systems.

Read more here: https://t.co/SlURte3EC9

#Photonics #Lasers

核心总结：Sivers Photonics（$SIVE）是 CPO（共封装光学）领域目前被市场严重低估的核心 CW 激光供应商，它已悄然深度映射到 Apple、AMD、Marvell Celestial、Amazon、Microsoft、Meta 等 hyperscaler 的供应链，并通过 Jabil、POET、Ayar Labs、O-Net、Lightmatter、Lightelligence 等关键伙伴形成广泛覆盖，涵盖可插拔 1.6T/3.2T 模块和共封装光学两大路线。作为激光这一 AI 数据中心光互连的瓶颈环节，SIVE 将直接受益于 2027-2028 年 CPO 超级周期的爆发式增长，具有极高的 TAM 扩张和业绩爆发潜力，目前多数高端客户关系仍未被市场充分认知。

text:
CPO Supply Chain Participants (Bank of America):

$NVDA: 
- Two CPO-based systems: Quantum-X InfiniBand Photonics + Spectrum-X Ethernet Photonics. 

$AVGO:
- Currently on 3rd generation of CPO-enabled switch ASIC w/ the TH6 Davisson released in H2'25

$MRVL: 
- Competes in CPO for both its XPU + switch ASIC businesses.
- Celestial acquisition gives stronger LT foothold in CPO; $AMZN is the first hyperscaler design-win for their PF chiplets.

$LITE: 
- In a great position to take advantage of the CPO inflection given its capabilities in optical components, In-house InP production.
- Multi-yr supply agreement w/ Nvidia in exchange for $2B equity is in the multibillion dollar sales range through 2030.

$COHR:
- Expects a $5B TAM for CPO.
- Received $2B equity from Nvidia in exchange for a multi-billion CPO supply agreement starting CY27 + going through CY30 (lasers, modules, etc.).

$BESI (BE Semiconductor):
- Leader in hybrid bonding + is exposed to CPO via its advanced die-attach and packaging solutions which are critical for integrating photonic chips w/ switch ASICs in CPO architectures.
- Besi noted that Nvidia + Broadcom switches use multiple hybrid bonding processes.

$AIXA (Aixtron):
- 90% share in epi equipment for optoelectronics (lasers + SiPh devices) for high-speed data communication.

$SOI (Soitec):
- Integrates photonic components on a silicon-on-insulator (SOI) + provides significant benefits for optical transceivers.
- E.g. faster data rates, cost efficiency, power savings, smaller size + better signal integrity.

Note: the above are direct summaries/quotes from BofA's report.

Total Optical TAM expansion of 5x:
2025 = $14B
2030 = $73B

That matches to Goldman's low-end CPO scenario - based on $NVDA Vera Rubin / https://t.co/7MtIiFxLcV

Insatiable demand, tightly constrained supply.     

The rush to upgrade optical networking between data centers, plus shortages of critical subcomponents, have left $CIEN with a $7b reported backlog in Q1 2026. 

Incredibly... 

that is ~146% of FY2025 revenue. https://t.co/ahQWyxgrMh

text:
let's reason from first principles on what this list is actually saying.

$AXTI up 761%. #SNDK up 523%. #AAOI up 421%. #AEHR up 371%. #NVTS up 310%.

this is not a meme list. this is a semiconductor materials and photonics sweep. every single name in the top 10 touches either compound semiconductors, optical interconnects, flash storage, or power electronics.

the market spent 2024 and 2025 arguing about which hyperscaler wins the ai race. meanwhile the actual winners were the people selling the shovels to build the picks to mine the gold.

#BE at 248%. #DOCN at 229%. #INTC at 225%. #MU at 163%.

even intel. INTEL. the company everyone wrote the obituary for. up 225%.

you'd have to actively avoid the data to have missed the infrastructure layer thesis. grok flagged compound semiconductors and optical interconnect as the underloved layer of the ai stack 18 months ago. the consensus was chasing software multiples.

the physics was always simple: you cannot move data at ai speeds without photonics. you cannot train at ai scale without memory bandwidth. #MU and #LITE were not mysteries. they were thermodynamics.

the analysts who were bearish on semis in early 2025 are very quiet right now. concerningly quiet.

text:
Real reason why $NVDA earnings matter so much tonight.

Not just the headline beat/miss.

It’s the forward commentary on:

• Rubin ramp
• Blackwell margins
• Quantum-X deployments
• Spectrum-X timing
• CPO / silicon photonics adoption
• HBM pricing & packaging constraints

Because Nvidia is the operating system of the entire AI infrastructure stack.

The biggest risk into earnings, in my opinion:

Margins.

If Rubin + GB300 ramps accelerate while HBM4 and advanced packaging costs stay elevated, the market may finally start questioning whether AI infra growth can continue without margin compression.

But if Nvidia guides cleanly above expectations again?

Then the AI capex supercycle is still alive — and semis probably squeeze even harder into H2.

It’s a macro event for the entire AI economy.

text:
$SIVE (Sivers Photonics) is my highest-conviction pick for the strongest stock appreciation in the entire photonics sector over the next 2–3 years.
Current price: ~45 SEK. Long-term target (2027–2028): 150–250 SEK (3–5x upside potential).

Sivers is a pure-play leader in high-performance CW DFB laser arrays; the critical light source enabling co-packaged optics (CPO) and high-speed pluggable modules for AI data centers.

Evidence and data backing the thesis:

AI interconnect demand explosion: As clusters scale to 800G/1.6T+, copper hits hard limits on heat, signal loss, and density. Optical interconnects are becoming mandatory, driving massive demand for lasers.

Strong ecosystem positioning: Partnerships and design wins with Ayar Labs (SuperNova), POET Technologies, Marvell Celestial, Jabil, and others. Recent CHIPS Act grant with BAE Systems and MIT Lincoln Lab adds U.S. government validation and non-dilutive funding.

Dual growth drivers: Primary: AI photonics. Secondary: defense, LiDAR, and satellite comms. This diversification reduces risk while the AI ramp accelerates.

Small-cap asymmetry: Low current revenue base means successful volume ramps can drive explosive growth. 

Analysts see significant revenue scaling as major CPO programs move into production in 2027–2028.

Qualitative edge: Sivers has spent years perfecting high-reliability lasers with the performance needed for AI-scale deployments. 

The combination of government backing, strong customer pipeline, and being European (reducing geopolitical risk) gives it a real moat in a market where supply chain security matters more every day.

Risks:
Execution on volume ramp and yield
Potential dilution if more capital is needed
Competition in a fast-moving space

$SIVE sits right in the middle of the laser chokepoint for the next wave of AI interconnects. The photonics transition is structural and inevitable; Sivers is one of the best-positioned small-caps to benefit with real customer traction and government support.

This one ages very well. Worth accumulating on weakness.

text:
laser / photonics sector looks like one of the highest-conviction 6-month setups in the entire ai stack right now.

copper is hitting its physical limits fast as clusters scale to 800g and 1.6t+. heat, signal loss, and density issues are forcing hyperscalers to switch to optical interconnects at rack and campus scale. 

the data backs it up hard:
1.6t optical modules are moving from lab to commercial deployment in 2026

optical content per gpu is expected to rise 1.3–1.5x as scale-out and scale-across networks expand

silicon photonics market is forecasted to grow from ~$2.86b in 2025 to $28.75b by 2034 (29%+ cagr), with the biggest ramp hitting in the next 2–3 years

names like $LITE, $SIVE, $COHR, and $GLW sit right in the middle of the laser and optical engine supply chain with real design wins and qualification momentum. 

the ones that can deliver high-volume, high-reliability lasers and transceivers are going to see strong re-rating as hyperscalers pull harder.

the short-term risk is execution and any delay in volume ramps, but the structural demand is undeniable. 
this is not narrative; it’s the next physical bottleneck after memory and power.

the laser/photonics sector has one of the cleanest asymmetric setup for the next 6 months in my book.

text:
$LITE’s OCS tech being called an “overnight success” after 10 years of R&D (with heavy involvement from $SLXN and $P4O)  is exactly how the best photonics plays look right now.

the CEO’s answer on Chinese competitors was sharp; they’ve been iterating for a decade while others are still catching up. high-powered lasers originally developed for undersea applications are now being repurposed for co-packaged optics, which is the direction hyperscalers are heading as clusters scale to 800G/1.6T+.

i’m biased that this is the kind of deep technical moat that actually matters in the interconnect layer. 

hyperscalers are hitting real limits on heat, signal loss, and density that copper can’t solve. the names that have spent years perfecting lasers and optical circuit switches keep the structural edge.

the comments asking about other long-term OCS players like Huber-Suhner ($HUBN) acquiring Polatis in 2016 are right to look for similar multi-year development stories. 

the ones calling it “mandatory reading” get that this is not hype; it’s real manufacturing and qualification work that takes a decade.

this cycle rewards the execution plays solving proven physical bottlenecks that hyperscalers can’t pause for. 

photonics and OCS are one of those constraints right now, and $LITE (with the ecosystem around it) sits right in the middle. the setup has legs. this one ages well.

text:
最近光模块很热，尤其是DSP，CPO等相关的新一代“光芯片”技术，引领整个光产业的股票狂飙

YTD 表现里 AAOI +441%、LITE +166%、COHR +97%——同一个板块，分化 4 倍

而接下来 12-24 个月真正定生死的是 CPO 时间表：

2026 H2：NVDA 的 Quantum-X / Spectrum-X Photonics 首批 scale-out CPO switch 量产
2027 H2：Rubin Ultra 整机 scale-up CPO 首批出货

这两个节点 TAM 完全不在一个量级。按 Lumentum 自己披露的数据，Rubin Ultra 架构里 scale-up:scale-out 光引擎比是 648:144，约 4.5:1。意思是市场现在 price in 的是 2026 H2 的 scale-out 故事，但真正的钱在 2027 H2 之后才开始进来——这是一年的时间错位。

这个错位会改变一件事的发生：现在追 AAOI 这种 capacity arbitrage 的标的，赚的是 2025-2026 的供应紧张钱；2027 之后真正的赢家是有结构性 IP moat 的——LITE 的 EML 激光器（全球市占 50-60%）和 MRVL 的 1.6T Ara DSP 平台（已经是行业事实标准）。传言 NVDA 已经在跟 Lumentum 要 2027 年 5000 万颗激光器产能，是当前 trajectory 的两倍。

视角站的更高来看——光模块本质上不是独立行情，是 NVDA $90B 资本部署的下游放大版：

COHR：NVDA 投 $2B + 5 个 CPO 产品线合作
LITE：NVDA 投 $2B + 多年采购承诺 + EML 激光器锁定
MRVL：NVDA 投 $2B + NVLink Fusion，optical FY27 guide +50% YoY
Corning：$6B Meta deal + 多年协议

NVDA 这套操作不是单点投资，是把整条光互联供应链（激光器 → DSP → transceiver → CPO 引擎 → 光纤）整体锁死。AMD、Google、AWS 想做对等的 rack-level 出货，光是补齐这条供应链就要 3-5 年。

操作上分层我是这么看的：

Tier 1（结构性持仓）：MRVL、COHR——NVDA 资本绑定 + 技术 moat 长，按住不动
Tier 2（NVDA capex 高 beta）：LITE、CRDO——单一产品强但暴露窄，单仓别超 5%
Tier 3（capacity 套利不是 moat 套利）：AAOI、FN、ALAB——估值已极端，等任何 10%+ 回调再说

最后一个最容易被忽略的点：光模块板块不是 NVDA 的对冲，是 NVDA 的杠杆。NVDA 涨 10%，光板涨 15-20%；NVDA 跌 10%，光板跌 20-30%。beta 1.5-2x 是双向的。

所以仓位上要意识到 NVDA + 光模块合计就是同一个押注，别超过 AI infra 配置的 70%。剩下 30% 应该留给 power（GEV、VRT）、cooling（MOD）、custom silicon（AVGO）这些和 NVDA 资本绑定较少的子赛道——这样组合内部才有真正的对冲。

text:
Before turning to photonics, I tried my luck in biotech. The field caught my interest quite strongly, and for about two years I read almost exclusively books in that area. Overall, I had a fairly good track record.

I don’t own any of the four stocks $QURE, $CMPS, $CAPR, or https://t.co/zgqEYAzfFg, anymore. Back then, all four were asymmetric bets for me, and contrary to the odds, three of them worked out very well (multibaggers), while even the fourth still ended up slightly positive.

A few weeks ago, I made another biotech investment where I see an asymmetric opportunity. Unfortunately, I can’t share it here, even though I’ve written a full write-up on it. The company is simply too small, and sharing it would likely drive the price up significantly. Ideally, it should re-rate for fundamental reasons, not because of attention or momentum. In cases like this, a paid Substack model would actually make sense.

Once the case plays out, either successfully or not, I’ll share it here. That way it will be documented whether my “biotech touch” is still intact.

Many people probably associate me primarily with photonics, but in reality I’m more of a generalist covering a range of different fields. I try to avoid having only a single hammer in the toolbox, otherwise every problem starts looking like a nail.
embedded_text: Bought $QURE last week. The FDA must be crazy not to approve their candidate for Huntington's disease. Together with $CAPR, $CMPS and $CMP.AX, these are four companies with very promising data that are very likely to receive approval within the next 1-2 years.

but I wanted to write something up because of the pictured quote inspired me * When I first invested in $LPTH over a year ago, my initial thesis was largely driven by geopolitical tailwinds, especially China’s germanium export restrictions and the implications for domestic supply chains. But the reason I’ve continued to hold and add more shares very quickly evolved. And one aspect that truly strengthened my conviction in my investment is the leadership of @skrubin. Under his direction, the company has very clearly positioned itself at the forefront of EO/IR systems, threat detection, and advanced optics... markets with durable, long-term demand driven by national security and technological acceleration. And this current company positioning is not solely a case of “preparation meeting opportunity” with BlackDiamond serving as an alternative to germanium at the right moment. While that was an important inflection point no doubt, what gives me greater confidence going forward now is how Sam is actively compounding that advantage. He’s iterating on the core technology with next-generation BDNL materials, expanding applications into air defense and space-based systems, strengthening relationships with prime contractors like $LMT, $LHX, $ESLT and Anduril, pursuing strategic M&A (G5 and AMI), and methodically moving the company further up the value chain. These distinctions truly matter as some leaders benefit from favorable macro conditions while others build enduring platforms on top of them. Sam is clearly doing the latter. He’s turning a geopolitical tailwind into a structural competitive advantage and that’s what sets up LightPath not just to participate in this current cycle, but to compound through it. I truly think that the quote “The secret to investing is to invest in unstoppable people building inevitable things” genuinely applies to @skrubin and it clearly comes across with every investor engagement he participates in and not because of any sort of hype, but because of his clarity, his strategic intent, and consistent execution. He’s one of the few CEOs whose every single interview, earnings call, and even social media update I genuinely look forward to as an investor. Leaders like Sam are how companies transform and flourish, and I truly believe that over the next 5–10 years, LightPath has the potential to become the next FLIR Systems (now part Teledyne) or Leonardo DRS."}}}, "rest_id": "2025957796156510217", "source": "Twitter Web App", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "24964", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}}, "rest_id": "2057884513276240309", "source": "Twitter for iPhone", "unmention_data": {}, "views": {"count": "2843", "state": "EnabledWithCount"}}

text:
It has now been two months since I published my first deep dive on $SIVE 

The stock is up 10x since then and, honestly, I think it is now massively overextended.

SEK 21.7B market cap on SEK 306M of revenue.
More than 70x sales.

I remain bullish on the long-term photonics / AI connectivity thesis, and I was probably one of the first public bulls talking about the company. But even then, the current valuation is absolutely astronomical.

At this point, the market is already pricing in a brutal revenue ramp, massive conversion from NRE into product revenue, and near-perfect execution over the coming years.

That said, I’m not fucking selling

text:
$COHR, $MTSI, $STM and Hamamatsu quietly dominating SpaceX’s lasercomm supply chain is another real-world proof point that photonics demand is expanding beyond terrestrial data centers.

the post maps the space-qualified chain perfectly; precision optics/coatings/EDFAs, high-speed drivers/TIAs, PAT control ASICs, and high-sensitivity detectors. 

flight heritage is the ultimate moat in this market, and these four are already winning the first generation.

this is bullish for the broader photonics layer. 

hyperscalers need reliable low-latency global connectivity for AI data centers, edge computing, and the full physical stack. starlink-type orbital networks solve real bottlenecks that terrestrial grids can’t fix fast enough. the same laser and optical engine technology that powers inter-satellite links has direct read-through to terrestrial CPO and pluggable ramps.

this cycle rewards the execution plays solving proven physical bottlenecks that hyperscalers literally can’t pause for. photonics (lasers, optics, detectors) is one of those constraints, and the space demand layer just adds another structural tailwind on top of the terrestrial AI buildout.

the setup has legs. this one ages well.

text:
Nvidia isn’t just selling the picks and shovels — it’s quietly buying the entire gold mine.
By dropping billions into optics, power, photonics, and AI cloud players, Jensen is locking down the physical bottlenecks that could slow AI scaling. This isn’t random VC money; it’s vertical control that funnels more demand straight back to Nvidia GPUs while building a deeper moat.
Classic platform power move: own the dependencies. Expect the flywheel to spin harder.​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​​

@ParadisLabs Just really interested in $PENG
1. Cause of the SKT connection
2. Is the larger contexts without buying more GPUs

Explained some here 
https://t.co/Rta4bZjx7p

The whole photonics memory thing is just retail doing retail
embedded_text:
From what I have read about $PENG we have the following:

→ CXL lets servers tap a shared pool of DRAM over a fast interconnect. 
→ KV cache is the running memory LLMs need during inference, it overflows GPU VRAM fast. 
Putting the KV cache in a CXL pool means longer contexts
